text: Welcome to Fragmented, a software developer podcast where we talk about building good software and becoming better developers.
speaker: SPEAKER_01

text: My name's Don Felker.
speaker: SPEAKER_01

text: And I'm Kaushik Gopal.
speaker: SPEAKER_01

text: Welcome to the show.
speaker: SPEAKER_01

text: Before we get started here today, I wanted to tell you about the relaunch of androidjobs.io.
speaker: SPEAKER_01

text: Android Jobs is a job board that I launched many years ago and I just recently relaunched it with a brand new design and on a brand new platform.
speaker: SPEAKER_01

text: If you're looking to hire an additional Android team member or you know of somebody that wants to hire an Android professional of any sort, that includes developers, designers,
speaker: SPEAKER_01

text: anything of that nature, then please let them know about androidjobs.io.
speaker: SPEAKER_01

speaker: SPEAKER_01

text: So please go to androidjobs.io and you can post a job for free.

speaker: SPEAKER_01

speaker: SPEAKER_01

text: Again, check it out at androidjobs.io.
speaker: SPEAKER_01

text: Everybody, welcome back to the show.

text: It's around web, but it's also around mobile.

text: So it's something I'm very passionate about and excited to talk about it.

text: user of rails for years just simply because for me it works it's if i want to build a web app i can literally scaffold something up and i can be focused on business problems in minutes and not have to worry about plumbing and it's one of the biggest reasons i love rails is it's just super productive i enjoy developing in it
speaker: SPEAKER_01

text: And one of the things that I've always been a proponent of, especially and very vocal of in the mobile community, is that I've always felt that web was going to win eventually.

text: Now, what does that look like?

text: I don't know.

speaker: SPEAKER_01

text: I don't know.
speaker: SPEAKER_01

text: And when, again, I'm always working in Rails, and I ended up bumping into something very recent that came out called Hotwire, which kind of just changed the paradigm.
speaker: SPEAKER_01

text: I mean, it's not really super complicated from what I read and understood, but it kind of brought back a lot of the old principles that we had a decade or plus ago.
speaker: SPEAKER_01

speaker: SPEAKER_01

text: And that led into some stuff with Android that you can be done with Hotwire.

text: and maybe kind of talk about things you can build with it, why you would want to build with it, you know, some of the limitations, if there's any benefits, cons, all that kind of stuff.
speaker: SPEAKER_01

text: So let's just kind of start from the top.
speaker: SPEAKER_01

text: Yeah, yeah.
speaker: SPEAKER_02

text: What is Hotwire?
speaker: SPEAKER_02

text: Yeah, Hotwire is an umbrella of a few different technologies, and Hotwire stands for HTML over the wire.
speaker: SPEAKER_02

text: Yeah.
speaker: SPEAKER_02

text: And so it's a mindset that we have at Basecamp.
speaker: SPEAKER_02

text: Whenever possible, we like to render UI on the server, which is quite different from a lot of web development approaches where JavaScript is doing all the work on the client and the server is simply passing data back and forth.
speaker: SPEAKER_02

text: So very similar to, I mean, mobile development, which is typically the case.
speaker: SPEAKER_02

text: You have tons and tons of code and business logic on the client in a lot of setups.
speaker: SPEAKER_02

text: I guess a bit of background is helpful at Basecamp.
speaker: SPEAKER_02

text: Basecamp is primarily a web company.
speaker: SPEAKER_02

text: It's background.
speaker: SPEAKER_02

text: It wasn't until I actually arrived that we formally formed a proper mobile team with Android and iOS.
speaker: SPEAKER_02

text: We had had some apps available before that, but it was just random people in the company were working on it without a ton of
speaker: SPEAKER_02

text: expertise in the area.
speaker: SPEAKER_02

text: So Basecamp was kind of winging it a little bit before we started having a dedicated mobile team.
speaker: SPEAKER_02

text: So we were kind of late to the game as far as that goes.
speaker: SPEAKER_02

text: And one thing you mentioned is being productive in Rails.
speaker: SPEAKER_02

text: And we have some of the most experienced Rails developers around at Basecamp since we drive a lot of the features that actually end up in Rails.
speaker: SPEAKER_02

text: over the years.
speaker: SPEAKER_02

text: It's amazing what just a handful of Rails developers can do compared to the mobile side of things.
speaker: SPEAKER_02

text: It really is staggering.
speaker: SPEAKER_02

text: Part of that is just the way the database layer, the modeling, the
speaker: SPEAKER_02

text: You render UI.
speaker: SPEAKER_02

text: It's a collection that works together.
speaker: SPEAKER_02

text: And it feels on mobile, it feels more so that you're kind of stringing things together a little bit.
speaker: SPEAKER_02

text: On mobile?
speaker: SPEAKER_02

text: Quite often.
speaker: SPEAKER_02

text: I mean, things have improved so much with Jetpack and so many other different technologies.
speaker: SPEAKER_02

text: But you're still kind of putting it all together yourself.
speaker: SPEAKER_02

text: And every Android app is going to look...
speaker: SPEAKER_02

text: very different from the next Android app.
speaker: SPEAKER_02

text: That seems to be pretty common based on my experience.
speaker: SPEAKER_02

text: Anyway, so that's the background about the type of company we are.
speaker: SPEAKER_02

text: And so Basecamp, the product, is a really large app.
speaker: SPEAKER_02

text: It's a really large web app.
speaker: SPEAKER_02

text: We have something somewhere close to 200 screens throughout the app.
speaker: SPEAKER_02

text: So that's everything from product features, like admin screens, payment screens, just a lot of different stuff.
speaker: SPEAKER_02

text: So you can imagine how large of a mobile team you would need.
speaker: SPEAKER_02

text: to build fully featured apps to do that.
speaker: SPEAKER_02

text: We would be huge.
speaker: SPEAKER_02

text: And Basecamp has never had more than two Android programmers and two iOS programmers at the same time.
speaker: SPEAKER_02

text: Oh, really?
speaker: SPEAKER_01

text: Yeah.
speaker: SPEAKER_02

text: And I don't think ever more than probably half a dozen Rails programmers at the same time.
speaker: SPEAKER_02

text: Yeah.
speaker: SPEAKER_02

text: So, yeah.
speaker: SPEAKER_02

text: So we've always done a lot with a little.
speaker: SPEAKER_02

text: And our approach with Hotwire is the culmination of our approach over the last five or six years, trying to get to a point where we've standardized and dimensionized everything we do to merge web and native more seamlessly.
speaker: SPEAKER_02

text: So...
speaker: SPEAKER_02

text: I'll talk a little bit about some of the parts of Hotwire just briefly.
speaker: SPEAKER_02

text: There's way too much to give a comprehensive overview, but just so people have a little bit of a taste.
speaker: SPEAKER_02

text: The main piece of Hotwire is turbo.
speaker: SPEAKER_02

text: Okay.
speaker: SPEAKER_01

text: Turbo is, think of it as a JavaScript library in your web app.
speaker: SPEAKER_02

text: And it is pretty much all about navigation.
speaker: SPEAKER_02

text: So it brings a lot of single-page application information.
speaker: SPEAKER_02

text: features to a Rails app or any app, really.
speaker: SPEAKER_02

text: But I just want to say Hotwire will work with any web app.
speaker: SPEAKER_02

text: It doesn't just need to be for Rails.
speaker: SPEAKER_02

text: But we have built Rails-specific extensions for
speaker: SPEAKER_02

text: for Hotwire.
speaker: SPEAKER_02

text: So for example, we have a demo app for our mobile app, and that's just built on a tiny web server.
speaker: SPEAKER_02

text: It's not a Rails app at all, but it still demos using everything.
speaker: SPEAKER_02

text: So Rails is kind of the...
speaker: SPEAKER_02

text: It was built in the Rails community.
speaker: SPEAKER_02

text: It's going to be part of a future version of Rails by default.
speaker: SPEAKER_02

text: They're not tightly coupled, but their relationship is strong there.
speaker: SPEAKER_02

text: Turbo is all about navigation in a single page application kind of way.
speaker: SPEAKER_02

text: So instead of every time you click a link on the web, what Turbo does in the background is it intercepts every link request.
speaker: SPEAKER_02

text: it will go fetch the contents of that request and it will update the webpage with the new contents in just swapping out the body with the new contents.
speaker: SPEAKER_02

text: So you're not actually using the browser's...
speaker: SPEAKER_02

text: technologies to navigate links turbo is handling all that on its own and that's a very that's a very simplified version of what it's doing but it allows us to do a lot of things like handle handle form submissions in uh more advanced ways handle redirects in particular ways
speaker: SPEAKER_02

text: There's just a lot of things that Turbo gives us.
speaker: SPEAKER_02

text: On the web, it improves speed.
speaker: SPEAKER_02

text: You don't have to...
speaker: SPEAKER_02

text: The browser isn't having to reparse your styles and CSS and other JavaScript assets.
speaker: SPEAKER_02

text: You're staying in the same page context.
speaker: SPEAKER_02

text: But you get all that for free, basically, just by installing Turbo in your web app.
speaker: SPEAKER_02

text: And then Turbo also lets you have...
speaker: SPEAKER_02

text: some hooks on the mobile side, which is a huge, huge benefit as well.
speaker: SPEAKER_02

text: So that's Turbo.
speaker: SPEAKER_02

text: We can set that aside for a moment.
speaker: SPEAKER_02

text: And then there's also Stimulus, which is another library under Hotwire.
speaker: SPEAKER_02

text: And Stimulus is a JavaScript convention-based library that lets you progressively enhance features for your web app.
speaker: SPEAKER_02

text: So we're very big believers in progressive enhancement at Basecamp.
speaker: SPEAKER_02

text: By default, most of our features work just by typical...
speaker: SPEAKER_02

text: If you turned off your JavaScript, most of the features would just work with normal link navigation, form submissions, everything.
speaker: SPEAKER_02

text: But Stimulus lets you sprinkle interactivity on top of your...
speaker: SPEAKER_02

text: DOM in a convention-based way.
speaker: SPEAKER_02

text: So it's really powerful, but that's what Stimulus does.
speaker: SPEAKER_02

text: And then there's Strata.
speaker: SPEAKER_02

text: And Strata is the final piece.
speaker: SPEAKER_02

text: And this is a mobile, specifically for mobile.
speaker: SPEAKER_02

text: And Strata is a bridge layer between web and native for communication.
speaker: SPEAKER_02

text: Okay.
speaker: SPEAKER_02

text: So that library is not currently open source yet, but we're going to do that probably later this year.
speaker: SPEAKER_02

text: Okay.
speaker: SPEAKER_02

text: So those are the three main, main parts.
speaker: SPEAKER_02

text: So I know that's really high level and people are probably not clear on what it is yet, but let's go to turbo.
speaker: SPEAKER_02

text: So turbo is where it gets really interesting with web and mobile.
speaker: SPEAKER_02

text: Okay.
speaker: SPEAKER_02

text: Turbo is the part, just to recap, is where it's replacing part of the actual HTML DOM when I click a link or something like that, right?
speaker: SPEAKER_01

text: Yeah, correct.
speaker: SPEAKER_02

text: So we have native libraries for Turbo for Android and iOS.
speaker: SPEAKER_02

text: And they are libraries that control the navigation in your app using the web and also using native as well.
speaker: SPEAKER_02

text: So let me give an example.
speaker: SPEAKER_02

text: So say you've got a Rails app with Hotwire installed and you've not done anything with mobile at all yet.
speaker: SPEAKER_02

text: So you've got a regular Rails app, right?
speaker: SPEAKER_01

text: Rails app and you've got Turbo on there.
speaker: SPEAKER_02

text: So if you take the Turbo library and plug in a URL to point to your web app...

text: Let me interject here.
speaker: SPEAKER_01

text: Is this a web library?
speaker: SPEAKER_01

text: Is this a mobile library?
speaker: SPEAKER_01

text: Or where are we at here?
speaker: SPEAKER_01

text: It's in Android.
speaker: SPEAKER_02

text: I'm talking about the Android library here.
speaker: SPEAKER_02

text: So let's say we've got a demo app in the library that you can see that shows the structure of the app.
speaker: SPEAKER_02

text: If you were just to replace in that demo app your web URL with the demo apps, it would just work.
speaker: SPEAKER_02

text: It would just work.
speaker: SPEAKER_02

text: You would load your initial screen and every time you visited a new link, it would pop a new fragment onto the stack.
speaker: SPEAKER_02

text: It would show that web view and it would create a native backstack like you would expect on mobile.
speaker: SPEAKER_02

text: If you're just using a straight web view, you're not going to get any of those features.
speaker: SPEAKER_02

text: If you just had a single activity with a web view in it, you're not going to get the same type of backstack.
speaker: SPEAKER_02

text: And I'm kind of like mind blown here.

speaker: SPEAKER_01

speaker: SPEAKER_01

speaker: SPEAKER_01

text: Everything about the Turbo library is URL-driven.
speaker: SPEAKER_02

text: The great thing about the web is it's URLs.
speaker: SPEAKER_02

text: You've got a permalink for everything, which is great.
speaker: SPEAKER_02

text: We've lost a lot of that on mobile.
speaker: SPEAKER_02

text: So if you're running a hotwire app with Turbo, Android, then you're going to still respect that URL-driven nature of navigation.
speaker: SPEAKER_02

text: And the way we do that is a path configuration file that's just JSON.
speaker: SPEAKER_02

text: So what that looks like in the Turbo library.
speaker: SPEAKER_02

text: So your Android app will have a path configuration file.
speaker: SPEAKER_02

text: We actually recommend having a path configuration file that's remote, like on your server.
speaker: SPEAKER_02

text: That way you can update it at any time.
speaker: SPEAKER_02

text: and you can actually modify your app's behavior remotely.
speaker: SPEAKER_02

text: So for example, one thing here, by the way, too, is we don't think it's necessarily a great experience to just have your web app, just plug in your URL into the Turbo Android library and ship that app.
speaker: SPEAKER_02

text: You can totally do that.
speaker: SPEAKER_02

text: But there are going to be areas of your app that are...
speaker: SPEAKER_02

text: really frequently used, like your home screen, like the landing screen of your app, or maybe some interactive screens, you should probably just write those natively if you want the highest fidelity, the best experience on those screens.
speaker: SPEAKER_02

text: So Turbo Android and Turbo iOS make it really easy to decide what screens are native and what screens are web.
speaker: SPEAKER_02

text: So...
speaker: SPEAKER_02

text: the path configuration file lets you say what URLs point to what fragments on Android or view controllers in iOS.
speaker: SPEAKER_02

text: Okay.
speaker: SPEAKER_02

text: So, for example, let's say you have a... Let's say you have... Let's say your web team ships a new feature, and the URL is...
speaker: SPEAKER_02

text: literally slash feature.
speaker: SPEAKER_02

text: That's the path to the URL.
speaker: SPEAKER_02

text: So that shows that if you're running Turbo Android, then that feature will just show up in the app because it's a web app.
speaker: SPEAKER_02

text: And when you go to the slash feature URL, it will just load a web fragment by default.
speaker: SPEAKER_02

text: But now say the Android team wants to, they decide that it's a feature that's important enough to make native.
speaker: SPEAKER_02

text: you would create a native fragment in the app, and then you would point the URL slash feature to your new fragment, and then it would just work.
speaker: SPEAKER_02

text: So every time you tap a link or a button that goes to slash feature throughout your app, it will automatically load the correct fragment, and that will be native.
speaker: SPEAKER_02

text: Let's say you shipped a bug in the native screen.
speaker: SPEAKER_02

text: You could simply just update your path configuration file on your server and point it back to the web fragment.
speaker: SPEAKER_02

text: And you could do that seamlessly.
speaker: SPEAKER_02

text: That would be a temporary fix until you fixed your bug or whatever and got your new fragment out.
speaker: SPEAKER_01

text: Yeah, I mean, that's not necessarily a common use case or anything.
speaker: SPEAKER_02

text: But yeah, I'm just demonstrating how it can be used.

speaker: SPEAKER_02

speaker: SPEAKER_02

speaker: SPEAKER_02

text: Does that make sense?
speaker: SPEAKER_02

text: No, it completely... To me, as someone who's a very... A big proponent of the web, this makes absolute sense because I can now ship the feature in the web.
speaker: SPEAKER_01

text: It just shows up and starts working on the mobile clients.
speaker: SPEAKER_01

text: Yes, I understand it's not native.
speaker: SPEAKER_01

text: I get it.
speaker: SPEAKER_01

text: And I think that's the thing that needs to be stated here.
speaker: SPEAKER_01

text: It's not native.
speaker: SPEAKER_01

text: It might act like native with the backstack, which is fantastic.
speaker: SPEAKER_01

text: But it allows me to kind of get that feature out there.
speaker: SPEAKER_01

text: And then I can focus my time, my native development skills on the core components, which are going to give me the most bang for the buck, which is going to be the most valuable.
speaker: SPEAKER_01

text: It's kind of the question I actually have now is, do you have any set of heuristics that you follow to determine what screens should be native versus what should not?
speaker: SPEAKER_01

text: Is there any guidance you can give?
speaker: SPEAKER_01

text: I know it's going to be generalized.
speaker: SPEAKER_01

text: Yeah.
speaker: SPEAKER_01

text: Yeah.
speaker: SPEAKER_02

text: For us, to be honest, it's a gut feel for us.
speaker: SPEAKER_02

text: But I will say, we've gotten a lot better as a company at shipping web screens that look natural on mobile.
speaker: SPEAKER_02

text: The Hey email app is a great example of that.
speaker: SPEAKER_02

text: Yeah.
speaker: SPEAKER_02

text: Unless you're really trying to dig in and figure it out, you'd be hard-pressed to tell what screens are web and what's native.
speaker: SPEAKER_02

text: Oh, really?
speaker: SPEAKER_01

text: That's cool.
speaker: SPEAKER_01

text: Yeah.
speaker: SPEAKER_02

text: And it's not a thing that feedback that we get from customers.
speaker: SPEAKER_02

text: Hey, why is this screen web and this screen native?
speaker: SPEAKER_02

text: We literally never hear that feedback.
speaker: SPEAKER_02

text: So we've gotten pretty good at... Because really, unless a screen is quite interactive...
speaker: SPEAKER_02

text: a lot of screens are just displaying information.
speaker: SPEAKER_02

text: And those screens are the ones that are perfectly fine being web.
speaker: SPEAKER_02

text: The more interactivity you have, the more I think you need to think about converting that to a native screen.
speaker: SPEAKER_02

text: If you want to have gestures, if you want to have swipeable rows, that type of stuff, you're going to need to do that natively.
speaker: SPEAKER_02

text: But there are a lot of apps out there that have screens that you just need to present information and maybe have a couple buttons on there.
speaker: SPEAKER_02

text: And those take you to other screens.
speaker: SPEAKER_02

text: So those are the ones that I would say keep web.
speaker: SPEAKER_02

text: And your landing screen hopefully should be important.
speaker: SPEAKER_02

text: If your landing screen isn't important in your app, you may want to consider redesigning your app.
speaker: SPEAKER_02

text: Yeah.
speaker: SPEAKER_02

text: But that's always a great one to make native just because you can cache the data in a way that you open up the app and it's all there even if you're offline, for example.
speaker: SPEAKER_02

text: So that would be another area.
speaker: SPEAKER_02

text: When we first started, all these technologies were extractions from building Basecamp and Hay.
speaker: SPEAKER_02

text: and figuring out what worked and what didn't.
speaker: SPEAKER_02

text: And I always had the tendency to want to nativize everything.
speaker: SPEAKER_02

text: And I'm a mobile developer.
speaker: SPEAKER_02

text: That's where my bias is.
speaker: SPEAKER_02

text: But as I've been here longer, the thing that I've realized is actually it's worth investing in making the web screens look more natural on mobile.
speaker: SPEAKER_02

text: We can...
speaker: SPEAKER_02

text: change them immediately on the server.
speaker: SPEAKER_02

text: We can fix bugs easier.
speaker: SPEAKER_02

text: We can support them for the long term easier.
speaker: SPEAKER_02

text: One thing at Basecamp that we do is we have apps from 15 years ago that we're still running for customers that we haven't added any new features, but we're still running them.
speaker: SPEAKER_02

text: And so we're very much in the mindset of...
speaker: SPEAKER_02

text: building apps that we can maintain for the long term.
speaker: SPEAKER_02

text: And having dozens and dozens or hundreds of screens in a mobile app is really hard to maintain, especially with small teams.
speaker: SPEAKER_02

text: I've been there.
speaker: SPEAKER_01

text: It's very hard.
speaker: SPEAKER_01

text: Yeah, yeah.
speaker: SPEAKER_02

text: So, yeah, so it's...
speaker: SPEAKER_02

text: If you want to have that seamless integration, to me, it sounds like you probably have, and I'm just guessing here, at Basecamp and at Hay, you probably have a design language that you follow on both the native and web platforms.
speaker: SPEAKER_01

text: So it looks seamless to the end user that...
speaker: SPEAKER_01

text: this native button looks exactly like the web button.
speaker: SPEAKER_01

text: Is that how you kind of help bridge that gap for the end users so they don't see this battleship gray button versus some purple button on the website?
speaker: SPEAKER_01

text: Yeah, Hay is there.
speaker: SPEAKER_02

text: We were very intentional about that when building Hay.
speaker: SPEAKER_02

text: Basecamp 3, which is the current version...
speaker: SPEAKER_02

text: goes back five or six years now.
speaker: SPEAKER_02

text: And so we weren't as disciplined about that then, but we've gotten a lot better and we're actively working on that now.
speaker: SPEAKER_02

text: So bringing all the Hotwire features...
speaker: SPEAKER_02

text: back to Basecamp 3.
speaker: SPEAKER_02

text: Really, the first opportunity we got to use everything fresh was with Hay.
speaker: SPEAKER_02

text: There's nothing like a fresh start that lets you reevaluate how you're building things.
speaker: SPEAKER_02

text: start with a clean slate.
speaker: SPEAKER_02

text: We're in the middle of bringing a lot of the design cohesiveness back into Basecamp.
speaker: SPEAKER_02

text: That's our goal.
speaker: SPEAKER_02

text: You'll see that the designs are very similar.
speaker: SPEAKER_02

text: We worked with the web team and the mobile teams together to have...
speaker: SPEAKER_02

text: UI paradigms that are very similar.
speaker: SPEAKER_02

text: But also, it's not like the Android app looks like the iOS app.
speaker: SPEAKER_02

text: We customize it so it looks natural on the platform as well.
speaker: SPEAKER_02

text: Okay.
speaker: SPEAKER_01

text: Speaking of platforms, one of the questions that I had here too is, since I have an Android app and it's running native code, can I subscribe and have native notifications for my mobile app?
speaker: SPEAKER_01

text: Is that possible?
speaker: SPEAKER_01

text: You would have to do that all natively.
speaker: SPEAKER_02

text: So that would be something that we don't give any tools for that in Hotwire or anything.
speaker: SPEAKER_02

text: So yeah, those are the things too that that's part of the reason why you want a native app.
speaker: SPEAKER_02

text: There are all these hooks into the OS and different things that really make a native app feel natural on the platform.
speaker: SPEAKER_02

text: And Hotwire...
speaker: SPEAKER_02

text: And Turbo do not give you those hooks.
speaker: SPEAKER_02

text: Google does a great job giving you tools to build those.
speaker: SPEAKER_02

text: And a lot of those setups can be quite complex.
speaker: SPEAKER_02

text: So what's your push notification provider?
speaker: SPEAKER_02

text: We're fine saying that all the hooks into the OS, you need to write native code for that, just like you normally would.
speaker: SPEAKER_02

text: Okay.
speaker: SPEAKER_01

text: So I know that I purchased a Hay account a while back, and I get native notifications.
speaker: SPEAKER_01

text: And as you had stated, Hay is a hotwire-based app.
speaker: SPEAKER_01

text: How does that work from the Android perspective?
speaker: SPEAKER_01

text: It's going to be native, so you've written that native notification code.
speaker: SPEAKER_01

text: And when someone...
speaker: SPEAKER_01

text: I get a new email, perhaps...
speaker: SPEAKER_01

text: and I tap on that notification, how are you directing the user to go to the correct screen if it's URL-based?
speaker: SPEAKER_01

text: How does that work?
speaker: SPEAKER_01

text: Yeah, it's quite easy.
speaker: SPEAKER_02

text: So you're going to launch an intent with the URL in there.
speaker: SPEAKER_02

text: It's going to open up your activity and it will turbo... You do need to write the code to say, hey, is there an intent URL provided?
speaker: SPEAKER_02

text: Just like you normally would in an activity.
speaker: SPEAKER_02

text: If there is, we just...
speaker: SPEAKER_02

text: Literally, it's a one-line call.
speaker: SPEAKER_02

text: You just call navigate to that URL.
speaker: SPEAKER_02

text: And Turbo, the library is going to take care of navigating to the correct screen, whether it's web or native, based on your path configuration setup.
speaker: SPEAKER_02

text: Nice.
speaker: SPEAKER_01

text: So it'll automatically do that.
speaker: SPEAKER_02

text: It'll add that URL into the backstack if you want.
speaker: SPEAKER_02

text: It's really easy.
speaker: SPEAKER_02

text: If you wanted to clear the backstack before you navigate it, that's super easy as well.
speaker: SPEAKER_02

text: I should say Turbo Android library is built on Google's navigation framework library.
speaker: SPEAKER_02

text: It is a wrapper around that.
speaker: SPEAKER_02

text: So Google has a capability of associating fragments with URIs.
speaker: SPEAKER_02

text: Mm-hmm.
speaker: SPEAKER_02

text: There's a deep link feature for the navigation library.
speaker: SPEAKER_02

text: And what you're doing in the path configuration file is actually assigning one or more URL paths to a URI that maps to your fragment.
speaker: SPEAKER_02

text: Okay.
speaker: SPEAKER_01

text: So the nice thing is you're going to have a single activity architecture with the navigation library.
speaker: SPEAKER_02

text: The navigation library, well, you're going to use Turbo Android directly, but it is just sitting right on top of Google's navigation library.
speaker: SPEAKER_02

text: And there's lots of things that we do in there to make URL navigation dead simple and automatic.
speaker: SPEAKER_02

text: Google's navigation library, one of the criticisms has been, it felt very rigid.
speaker: SPEAKER_02

text: You have to define, okay, this screen maps to this screen.
speaker: SPEAKER_02

text: And you essentially create these hard links between
speaker: SPEAKER_02

text: one screen to another.
speaker: SPEAKER_02

text: When you have a web app that URLs can come from anywhere and navigate to anything, that doesn't work well at all.
speaker: SPEAKER_02

text: So Turbo Android takes care of mapping the URIs to the fragments and creating the back stack and all that stuff.
speaker: SPEAKER_02

text: That's nice.

text: So it kind of simplifies a lot of the approach for you that you don't have to do a lot of that plumbing work right out of the gate, right?

text: Yep.

speaker: SPEAKER_01

text: So this is actually where Strata comes in a little bit.
speaker: SPEAKER_02

text: So, yeah.
speaker: SPEAKER_02

text: So Strata is, so we've talked about writing all native screens or all web screens.
speaker: SPEAKER_02

text: Strata lets you have kind of the best of both.
speaker: SPEAKER_02

text: So let's say you have a web screen.
speaker: SPEAKER_02

text: I'll give a simple example first.
speaker: SPEAKER_02

text: So a standard paradigm for a lot of Android apps and iOS apps to have, if you've got a screen that's a form, in the top right corner, you'll have a submit button for that form up in the toolbar.
speaker: SPEAKER_02

text: You can't do that if you just have a web screen because you've got a native toolbar.
speaker: SPEAKER_02

text: You can't put a submit button up there.
speaker: SPEAKER_02

text: What Strata lets you do is mark up, add attributes to your DOM elements.
speaker: SPEAKER_02

text: your web screen and when that screen loads it will find it will it will see the markup and it will find components that you've registered in your android app so for example in my android app i say i support a form component and i know that form components have a submit button
speaker: SPEAKER_02

text: So when Strata on the web and the screen loads, when that screen loads, Strata is going to say, hey, there's a form component on this screen.
speaker: SPEAKER_02

text: And here's the details about that form.
speaker: SPEAKER_02

text: So for example, say here's the title of the submit button.
speaker: SPEAKER_02

text: And it will pass a message to the Android app.
speaker: SPEAKER_02

text: So let's say the title of the submit button is post.
speaker: SPEAKER_02

text: So the form component on Android will add a toolbar button and give it the title post.
speaker: SPEAKER_02

text: When the user clicks that, so you're on a web view screen, when the user clicks the native post button, we pass the message that we received back to Strata on the web, and it will submit the form.
speaker: SPEAKER_02

text: for the button associated with the native button that we just pressed.
speaker: SPEAKER_02

text: So it'll submit the form for you, and then the form submission will submit to the server, the server will send back a URL, and then the Android app will navigate to wherever the server tells you to after the form submission is complete.
speaker: SPEAKER_02

text: So that's one example, actually a pretty simple example of what Strata does and how you can blend native and web.
speaker: SPEAKER_02

text: You can put UI Chrome anywhere.
speaker: SPEAKER_02

text: On your web screen.
speaker: SPEAKER_02

text: So for example, on Hey, we have...
speaker: SPEAKER_02

text: When you're looking at an email, that's a web view.
speaker: SPEAKER_02

text: But at the bottom, there's an action bar that has some buttons.
speaker: SPEAKER_02

text: That's all native.
speaker: SPEAKER_02

text: And when you tap that, that's just...
speaker: SPEAKER_02

text: When you tap it, it tells the web, Hey, I tap this button.
speaker: SPEAKER_02

text: The web will perform whatever action it needs to.
speaker: SPEAKER_02

text: And then it will...
speaker: SPEAKER_02

text: perform a navigation or whatever your web app is telling it to do.
speaker: SPEAKER_02

text: One thing I should say Strata is great for is the quickest way to break the illusion of a web app not feeling native is if you have pop-ups and menus on the web.
speaker: SPEAKER_02

text: That is a perfect way to break the illusion of the screen not feeling native.
speaker: SPEAKER_02

text: So for example, and hey, whenever you tap, whenever we show a button, or sorry, whenever we show a menu, Strata, we actually do that through Strata and show a bottom sheet or show a pop-up.
speaker: SPEAKER_02

text: And so you get those native dialogues and it feels very native.
speaker: SPEAKER_02

text: But...
speaker: SPEAKER_02

text: We don't have to have any business logic in the Android app at all.
speaker: SPEAKER_02

text: I think that's one of the really big, powerful things about our approach is we have very, very little business logic in our apps.
speaker: SPEAKER_02

text: Because every time we have a web screen, that was rendered on the server.
speaker: SPEAKER_02

text: And if we want to add native features, Strata is just describing UI to show.
speaker: SPEAKER_02

text: And the Android app doesn't need to know any business logic because the server is already deciding whether that button should be visible, what users have access to it, down the line.
speaker: SPEAKER_02

text: So I think that's one of the powerful things is keeping your business logic...
speaker: SPEAKER_02

text: on the server is a really powerful mindset.
speaker: SPEAKER_02

text: As a mobile developer, have you found that to be challenging?

text: I think the reason why I ask is because I feel like for me, it's just natural to want to just solve something in code.
speaker: SPEAKER_01

text: Have you had to change your mindset around how you're developing maybe about, hey, oh, I need to actually handle this on the server versus on the client?
speaker: SPEAKER_01

text: Yeah.
speaker: SPEAKER_02

text: Yeah.

text: I have had to change my mindset for sure.

speaker: SPEAKER_02

text: seeing how much easier it is, for example, in Rails, to keep business logic there and keep the Android app, the client, as simple as possible.
speaker: SPEAKER_02

speaker: SPEAKER_02

speaker: SPEAKER_02

text: Turbo creates a single web page, single page application, but we're actually loading different fragments.
speaker: SPEAKER_02

text: So what we do is we actually swap the web view between fragments as you're navigating the back stack.
speaker: SPEAKER_02

text: So we have a single shared web view.
speaker: SPEAKER_02

text: So you don't need to load the full page every time on each screen.
speaker: SPEAKER_02

text: So when you go from page A to page B, we're actually Snapchatting the DOM.
speaker: SPEAKER_02

text: It's put in a cache in the web view.
speaker: SPEAKER_02

text: And when you visit page B, we'll load the new contents.
speaker: SPEAKER_02

text: Turbo will load that into the body.
speaker: SPEAKER_02

text: And then when you hit the back button, it will actually load the...
speaker: SPEAKER_02

text: snapshotted contents from the previous page directly into the into the web view so it's uh super fast like that oh wow that's uh well it matters quite a bit too because that's one of the big problems with people have with web apps with wrappers is it just seems really slow and clunky and it seems like you're getting around that with this caching that's pretty cool
